g.data-narration: Welcome to Transaction Processing in JDBC. Learn to manage database operations atomically for data integrity.
g.text: ACID Properties, JDBC Methods, and Real-World Applications

g.data-narration: A transaction is a logical unit containing multiple database operations that must execute as a single atomic unit.
g.text: A logical unit of work containing multiple SQL operations (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)
that must ALL succeed or ALL fail together to maintain data integrity.

g.data-narration: Without transactions, partial updates cause data inconsistency. Consider transferring money between bank accounts atomically.
g.text: Real-world scenario: Transfer ₹5000 from Account A to Account B

g.data-narration: ACID ensures reliable database transactions: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability work together.
g.text: Four essential guarantees that ensure reliable database transactions:

g.data-narration: Atomicity guarantees all-or-nothing execution. Either all operations succeed and commit, or if any fails, the entire transaction rolls back.
Consistency ensures the database moves from one valid state to another, maintaining all integrity constraints and rules.
g.text: Database must satisfy all constraints before and after transaction commit.

g.data-narration: Isolation controls how concurrent transactions affect each other. Different isolation levels prevent various read anomalies.
g.text: Four isolation levels prevent different types of read anomalies:

g.data-narration: Durability guarantees committed transactions survive system failures through write-ahead logging and recovery mechanisms.
g.text: Committed data is permanently saved and survives system crashes.
Log written BEFORE data changes → Recover on crash

g.data-narration: JDBC provides four core methods: setAutoCommit, commit, rollback, and setSavepoint for transaction control.
g.text: Four essential methods from the Connection interface:

g.data-narration: Every transaction follows a lifecycle: begin, execute operations, then either commit on success or rollback on failure.
Always use try-catch-finally for proper transaction handling. Restore auto-commit in finally to prevent connection leaks.
Savepoints create intermediate rollback points within a transaction, enabling partial rollback for complex multi-stage operations.
g.text: Create intermediate rollback points within transactions:

g.data-narration: Bank transfer example: atomically withdraw from one account and deposit to another using prepared statements.
g.text: Transfer ₹5000 from Account 101 to 102 - must be atomic:

g.data-narration: E-commerce order example: update inventory for each item and create order record atomically within a single transaction.
g.text: Create order while atomically updating inventory:
UPDATE inventory SET stock = stock - ? WHERE item_id = ? AND stock >= ?
// If rows=0, throw StockException → triggers rollback

g.data-narration: A transaction moves through five states: Active, Partially Committed, Committed, Failed, and Aborted based on operations.
Avoid common mistakes: forgetting setAutoCommit, rollback after commit, wrong isolation levels, and not releasing savepoints.
Key takeaways: ACID ensures reliability, setAutoCommit(false) starts transactions, commit/rollback control outcomes, savepoints enable partial rollback.
Exam tips: Remember ACID mnemonic, auto-commit defaults to true, know isolation level tradeoffs, and practice transaction code templates.
g.text: • ACID Mnemonic: "Always Cement Important Details" (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability)
• Auto-Commit: JDBC defaults to true - remember to disable for transactions
• Isolation Levels: Higher isolation = less anomalies but more locking/performance impact
• Code Practice: Memorize the try-catch-finally transaction template
